[[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese burial practices]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[ja:横穴墓]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun forks]][[Category:kk]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:kk]] {{Kofun navbar]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:fixing the kk issue]] [[category:Sōzenji_Yokoguchi_Cluster forks]]

{{ill|Sōzenji|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺]]. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|en}}[[ja:郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site]], believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|en}}[[ja:南小泉遺跡]], a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[dromos|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺 (仙台市)]] was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|en}}[[ja:宮沢橋 (広瀬川)]], excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|en}}[[ja:広瀬河畔通り]] was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|en}}[[ja:宮沢橋 (広瀬川)]], excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|en}}[[ja:広瀬河畔通り]] was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

[[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese burial practices]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[ja:横穴墓]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun forks]][[Category:kk]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:kk]] {{Kofun navbar]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:fixing the kk issue]] [[category:Sōzenji_Yokoguchi_Cluster forks]]

{{ill|Sōzenji|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺]]. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|en}}[[ja:郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site]], believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|en}}[[ja:南小泉遺跡]], a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[dromos|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺 (仙台市)]] was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|en}}[[ja:宮沢橋 (広瀬川)]], excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|en}}[[ja:広瀬河畔通り]] was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

[[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese burial practices]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[ja:横穴墓]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun forks]][[Category:kk]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:kk]] {{Kofun navbar]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:fixing the kk issue]] [[category:Sōzenji_Yokoguchi_Cluster forks]]

{{ill|Sōzenji|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺]]. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|en}}[[ja:郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site]], believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|en}}[[ja:南小泉遺跡]], a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[dromos|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺 (仙台市)]] was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|en}}[[ja:宮沢橋 (広瀬川)]], excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|en}}[[ja:広瀬河畔通り]] was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|en}}[[ja:宮沢橋 (広瀬川)]], excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|en}}[[ja:広瀬河畔通り]] was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

[[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese burial practices]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[ja:横穴墓]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun forks]][[Category:kk]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:kk]] {{Kofun navbar]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:fixing the kk issue]] [[category:Sōzenji_Yokoguchi_Cluster forks]]

{{ill|Sōzenji|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺]]. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|en}}[[ja:郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site]], believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|en}}[[ja:南小泉遺跡]], a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[dromos|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺 (仙台市)]] was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|en}}[[ja:宮沢橋 (広瀬川)]], excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|en}}[[ja:広瀬河畔通り]] was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

[[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese burial practices]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[ja:横穴墓]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun forks]][[Category:kk]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:kk]] {{Kofun navbar]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:fixing the kk issue]] [[category:Sōzenji_Yokoguchi_Cluster forks]]

{{ill|Sōzenji|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺]]. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|en}}[[ja:郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site]], believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|en}}[[ja:南小泉遺跡]], a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[dromos|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺 (仙台市)]] was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|en}}[[ja:宮沢橋 (広瀬川)]], excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|en}}[[ja:広瀬河畔通り]] was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|en}}[[ja:宮沢橋 (広瀬川)]], excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|en}}[[ja:広瀬河畔通り]] was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

[[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese burial practices]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[ja:横穴墓]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun forks]][[Category:kk]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]] {{Kofun navbar]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:fixing the kk issue]] [[category:Sōzenji_Yokoguchi_Cluster forks]]

{{ill|Sōzenji|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺]]. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|en}}[[ja:郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site]], believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|en}}[[ja:南小泉遺跡]], a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[dromos|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺 (仙台市)]] was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|en}}[[ja:宮沢橋 (広瀬川)]], excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|en}}[[ja:広瀬河畔通り]] was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

[[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese burial practices]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[ja:横穴墓]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun forks]][[Category:kk]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]] {{Kofun navbar]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:fixing the kk issue]] [[category:Sōzenji_Yokoguchi_Cluster forks]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Sōzenji]][[Category:qq]]

{{ill|Sōzenji|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺]]. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|en}}[[ja:郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site]], believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|en}}[[ja:南小泉遺跡]], a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[dromos|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺 (仙台市)]] was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|en}}[[ja:宮沢橋 (広瀬川)]], excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|en}}[[ja:広瀬河畔通り]] was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

{{ill|Sōzenji|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺]]. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|en}}[[ja:郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site]], believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|en}}[[ja:南小泉遺跡]], a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[dromos|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.

The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|en}}[[ja:宮沢橋 (広瀬川)]], excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|en}}[[ja:広瀬河畔通り]] was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

[[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese burial practices]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[ja:横穴墓]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Corridor-type kofun forks]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]] {{Kofun navbar]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:fixing the kk issue]] [[category:Sōzenji_Yokoguchi_Cluster forks]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Sōzenji]][[Category:qq]]

{{ill|Sōzenji|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺]]. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|en}}[[ja:郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site]], believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|en}}[[ja:南小泉遺跡]], a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[dromos|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺 (仙台市)]] was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|en}}[[ja:宮沢橋 (広瀬川)]], excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|en}}[[ja:広瀬河畔通り]] was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

=== Chūgoku Region === * {{ill|Jūōmen Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:十王免横穴群]] ({{ill|Matsue|en}}[[ja:松江市]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]) - 37 tombs * {{ill|Kitsunedani Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:狐谷横穴墓群]] (Matsue, Shimane) - 16 tombs * {{ill|Kamikonishi Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:上塩冶横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Izumo, Shimane|en}}[[ja:出雲市]], Shimane) * {{ill|Takatsuku Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:高津久横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Chibu, Shimane|en}}[[ja:知夫村]], [[Oki District]])
=== Kyushu Region === * {{ill|Takenami Site|en}}[[ja:竹並遺跡]] ({{ill|Yukuhashi|en}}[[ja:行橋市]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]) - Over 1,000 tombs * {{ill|Shiroyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:城山横穴群]] ({{ill|Fukuchi, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:福智町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Mizumachi Site|en}}[[ja:水町遺跡]] ({{ill|Nōgata|en}}[[ja:直方市]], Fukuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, over 40 tombs * {{ill|Kozuki Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:古月横穴]] ({{ill|Kurate, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:鞍手町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Nagino Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫ナギノ横穴群]] ({{ill|Tamana|en}}[[ja:玉名市]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Anakanon Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫穴観音横穴群]] (Tamana, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nabeta Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:鍋田横穴群]] ({{ill|Yamaga, Kumamoto|en}}[[ja:山鹿市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 60 tombs * {{ill|Ōmura Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:大村横穴群]] ({{ill|Hitoyoshi|en}}[[ja:人吉市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 27 tombs * {{ill|Yokkaichi Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:四日市横穴群]] ({{ill|Usa|en}}[[ja:宇佐市]], [[Ōita Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 161 tombs * {{ill|Takio Hyakka Yokoguchi Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝尾百穴横穴古墳群]] ({{ill|Ōita, Ōita|en}}[[ja:大分市]], Ōita) - Municipal Historic Site, 75 tombs * {{ill|Hasugaike Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:蓮ヶ池横穴群]] ({{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en}}[[ja:宮崎市]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site
== Related Articles == * [[Kofun period]] * [[Decorated kofun|Decorated Kofun]]

{{Cite web|date=August 14, 2015 (Friday) 12:02 (UTC)}} {{Japanese Temple |Name = Sōzen-ji (宗禅寺) |Image = [[File:Souzenji-temple, Tokyo.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Hamura, Tokyo|ja|羽村市}}, Tokyo, Japan |Latitude = 35|Latitude_min = 45|Latitude_sec = 06.1 |Longitude = 139|Longitude_min = 19|Longitude_sec = 06.9 |Mountain_name = Iōzan (医王山)<ref name="overview"/> |Sect = Zen Buddhism, {{ill|Rinzai school|ja|臨済宗}} |Subsect = {{ill|Kencho-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗建長寺派}}<ref name="overview"/> |Principal_deity = Shakyamuni Buddha (釈迦牟尼佛像) <ref name="overview">{{Cite web|title=Overview of Sōzen-ji|url=http://www.tesshow.jp/tama/fssahamra/temple_hamra_sozen.html|website=Neko no Ashiato|language=Japanese|accessdate=2015-08-14}}</ref> |Founded = Genna 1 (1615)<ref name="overview"/> |Founder = Gyokushū Genkyū (玉岫玄球) <ref name="overview"/> |Patron = Residents of Kawasaki Village |Pilgrimage_site = {{ill|Okutama Pilgrimage New Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|奥多摩霊場新四国八十八ヶ所霊場}} - 33rd temple |Cultural_properties = Yakushi Hall (Hamura City Designated Tangible Cultural Property), Main Image: Seated Statue of Shakyamuni Buddha (Hamura City Designated Tangible Cultural Property)<ref name="a">{{Cite web|title=Sōzen-ji - Bashō's Haiku Monument|url=http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/urawa328/souzenji.html|language=Japanese|accessdate=2015-08-14}}</ref> |Official_HP = https://www.hamura-souzenji.com |Official_HP_name = Kenchō-ji School of Rinzai Zen - Sōzen-ji |Map2 = {{Location map|Japan Tokyo|label=Sōzen-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Sōzen-ji''' (宗禅寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai school|ja|臨済宗}} temple located in {{ill|Hamura, Tokyo|ja|羽村市}}, Tokyo. The temple’s mountain name is Iōzan (医王山).<ref name="overview"/> It is affiliated with {{ill|Kencho-ji|ja|建長寺}} in Kamakura as its head temple and {{ill|Kōtoku-ji (Akiruno)|ja|廣徳寺}} in Itsukaichi, Akiruno, as its branch temple.<ref name="official_site">Official website of Sōzen-ji.</ref>
Founded in 1615 (Genna 1),<ref name="overview"/> the temple welcomed the Zen master Gyokushū Genkyū from Kōtoku-ji as its founding abbot.<ref name="official_site"/> Originally located near the Tama River, it was relocated to its current site in 1695 due to flooding and the construction of the Tamagawa Aqueduct. Sōzen-ji was established as the {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}} (family temple) for the villagers of the former Kawasaki Village (now parts of Hamura: Kawasaki, Tamagawa, Shinmeidai, Futaba Town) under the policy of the Tokugawa shogunate.<ref name="official_site"/>
The Yakushi Hall predates the temple’s founding, and although the principal deity is Shakyamuni Buddha, the mountain name "Iōzan" refers to {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|ja|薬師如来}} (Medicine Buddha), highlighting its historical significance as the temple’s symbol.<ref name="official_site"/> An annual festival dedicated to Yakushi Nyorai is held on the first Saturday of October. The temple also serves as the 33rd site of the {{ill|Okutama Pilgrimage New Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|奥多摩霊場新四国八十八ヶ所霊場}}.
== Temple Grounds ==
- **Yakushi Hall (Kawasaki Ippongi-dō)** Rebuilt during the Muromachi period, it is considered the oldest structure in Hamura City. The hall, known as "Ippongi-dō" (Single Log Hall), is said to have been constructed from a single large driftwood log that washed ashore during a great flood, to honor the souls of those who perished.<ref name="a" />
- **Sansha-dō** (Hall of Three Shrines), **Jizō for Epidemic Dispersal**, **Kōshin Mound**, **Kyōzuka** (Sutra Mound), and **Bashō's Haiku Monument**.<ref name="a" />
== Events ==
=== Annual Events === - **February 3**: Setsubun Bean-Throwing Festival - **Spring Equinox Period**: Spring Cultural Exhibition - **April 8**: {{ill|Hanamatsuri|ja|花祭り}} (Buddha's Birthday) with Sweet Tea Offerings - **July 15**: {{ill|Ullambana Festival|ja|盂蘭盆会}} and Segaki Ceremony at the Main Gate - **1st Saturday of August**: Toro Nagashi (Lantern Floating Ceremony) at Miyano-shita Sports Park (event discontinued after 2022) - **1st Saturday of October**: Yakushi Nyorai Grand Festival - **December 31**: Joya no Kane (New Year’s Eve Bell Ringing)
=== Monthly Events === - **Shakyō (Sutra Copying) Gathering**: 1st, 11th, and 21st of each month, 1–3 PM - **Wood Carving Class**: 1st & 3rd Saturdays, 1–4 PM - **Morning Porridge Zazen Gathering**: 1st Sunday of every month, 6–8:30 AM<ref name="b">{{Cite web|title=Zazen Events in Tokyo|url=http://www.rinnou.net/cont_02/kenbetu/13t.html|language=Japanese|accessdate=2015-08-14}}</ref> - **Public Lectures**: Monthly - **Yoga Class**: 1st & 3rd Thursdays, 10–11 AM - **Real Terakoya**: 2nd Saturday mornings, hosted by [https://www.instagram.com/raimugihouse/ Raimugi House]
=== Weekly Events === - **Children’s Zazen Gathering**: Every Saturday, 6–7 PM<ref name="b" /> - **Adult Zazen Gathering**: Every Saturday, 7:30–8:30 PM<ref name="b" />
Additional activities include Buddhist chanting, shakuhachi (bamboo flute), haiku gatherings, handmade udon workshops, newspaper salons, and a "Heart Counseling Room" offering tarot therapy.
== Topics == - Since 2021, the theater group {{ill|Aquarium Theater|ja|水族館劇場}} has held performances on a special stage set up in the temple’s parking lot.
== Cultural Properties == - **Yakushi Hall (Kawasaki Ippongi-dō)**: Rebuilt during the Tenshō era (Muromachi period), designated as a Hamura City Cultural Property.<ref name="hamura">[http://www.city.hamura.tokyo.jp/0000001535.html Hamura City Cultural Properties], accessed March 12, 2021.</ref> - **Main Image: Seated Statue of Shakyamuni Buddha**, along with statues of Kāśyapa and Ānanda (Hamura City Designated Cultural Property).<ref name="hamura" />
== Access == - **By Train**: - 25-minute walk from {{ill|Fussa Station|ja|福生駅}} (JR Ome Line) or 12-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Hamura Station|ja|羽村駅}} - 8-minute taxi ride from Fussa Station - 25-minute walk or 8-minute taxi ride from {{ill|Higashi-Fussa Station|ja|東福生駅}} (JR Hachikō Line)
- **By Car**: - 25 minutes from Hachioji IC (Chūō Expressway) - 15 minutes from Hinode IC (Ken-Ō Expressway) - 20 minutes from Ōme IC (Ken-Ō Expressway)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite web|title=About Sōzen-ji|url=http://www.hamura-souzenji.com/about/|website=Sōzen-ji Official Website|accessdate=2021-03-02|ref={{sfnref|Official Site - About Sōzen-ji}}}} * {{cite book|title={{ill|New Edition Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}|volume=Vol. 108 Tama District No. 20|chapter=Kawasaki Village - Sōzen-ji, Yakushi Hall|url={{NDLJP|763992/52}}|publisher=Home Ministry Geography Bureau|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|New Edition Musashi Fudoki - Kawasaki Village}}}}

'''Sōzen-ji''' (宗禅寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai school|ja|臨済宗}} temple located in {{ill|Hamura, Tokyo|ja|羽村市}}, Tokyo. The temple’s mountain name is Iōzan (医王山).<ref name="overview"/> It is affiliated with {{ill|Kencho-ji|ja|建長寺}} in Kamakura as its head temple and {{ill|Kōtoku-ji (Akiruno)|ja|廣徳寺}} in Itsukaichi, Akiruno, as its branch temple.<ref name="official_site">Official website of Sōzen-ji.</ref>
Founded in 1615 (Genna 1),<ref name="overview"/> the temple welcomed the Zen master Gyokushū Genkyū from Kōtoku-ji as its founding abbot.<ref name="official_site"/> Originally located near the Tama River, it was relocated to its current site in 1695 due to flooding and the construction of the Tamagawa Aqueduct. Sōzen-ji was established as the {{ill|Bodaiji|ja|菩提寺}} (family temple) for the villagers of the former Kawasaki Village (now parts of Hamura: Kawasaki, Tamagawa, Shinmeidai, Futaba Town) under the policy of the Tokugawa shogunate.<ref name="official_site"/>
The Yakushi Hall predates the temple’s founding, and although the principal deity is Shakyamuni Buddha, the mountain name "Iōzan" refers to {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|ja|薬師如来}} (Medicine Buddha), highlighting its historical significance as the temple’s symbol.<ref name="official_site"/> An annual festival dedicated to Yakushi Nyorai is held on the first Saturday of October. The temple also serves as the 33rd site of the {{ill|Okutama Pilgrimage New Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|奥多摩霊場新四国八十八ヶ所霊場}}.
== Temple Grounds ==
- **Yakushi Hall (Kawasaki Ippongi-dō)** Rebuilt during the Muromachi period, it is considered the oldest structure in Hamura City. The hall, known as "Ippongi-dō" (Single Log Hall), is said to have been constructed from a single large driftwood log that washed ashore during a great flood, to honor the souls of those who perished.<ref name="a" />
- **Sansha-dō** (Hall of Three Shrines), **Jizō for Epidemic Dispersal**, **Kōshin Mound**, **Kyōzuka** (Sutra Mound), and **Bashō's Haiku Monument**.<ref name="a" />
== Events ==
=== Annual Events === - **February 3**: Setsubun Bean-Throwing Festival - **Spring Equinox Period**: Spring Cultural Exhibition - **April 8**: {{ill|Hanamatsuri|ja|花祭り}} (Buddha's Birthday) with Sweet Tea Offerings - **July 15**: {{ill|Ullambana Festival|ja|盂蘭盆会}} and Segaki Ceremony at the Main Gate - **1st Saturday of August**: Toro Nagashi (Lantern Floating Ceremony) at Miyano-shita Sports Park (event discontinued after 2022) - **1st Saturday of October**: Yakushi Nyorai Grand Festival - **December 31**: Joya no Kane (New Year’s Eve Bell Ringing)
=== Monthly Events === - **Shakyō (Sutra Copying) Gathering**: 1st, 11th, and 21st of each month, 1–3 PM - **Wood Carving Class**: 1st & 3rd Saturdays, 1–4 PM - **Morning Porridge Zazen Gathering**: 1st Sunday of every month, 6–8:30 AM<ref name="b">{{Cite web|title=Zazen Events in Tokyo|url=http://www.rinnou.net/cont_02/kenbetu/13t.html|language=Japanese|accessdate=2015-08-14}}</ref> - **Public Lectures**: Monthly - **Yoga Class**: 1st & 3rd Thursdays, 10–11 AM - **Real Terakoya**: 2nd Saturday mornings, hosted by [https://www.instagram.com/raimugihouse/ Raimugi House]
=== Weekly Events === - **Children’s Zazen Gathering**: Every Saturday, 6–7 PM<ref name="b" /> - **Adult Zazen Gathering**: Every Saturday, 7:30–8:30 PM<ref name="b" />
Additional activities include Buddhist chanting, shakuhachi (bamboo flute), haiku gatherings, handmade udon workshops, newspaper salons, and a "Heart Counseling Room" offering tarot therapy.
== Topics == - Since 2021, the theater group {{ill|Aquarium Theater|ja|水族館劇場}} has held performances on a special stage set up in the temple’s parking lot.
== Cultural Properties == - **Yakushi Hall (Kawasaki Ippongi-dō)**: Rebuilt during the Tenshō era (Muromachi period), designated as a Hamura City Cultural Property.<ref name="hamura">[http://www.city.hamura.tokyo.jp/0000001535.html Hamura City Cultural Properties], accessed March 12, 2021.</ref> - **Main Image: Seated Statue of Shakyamuni Buddha**, along with statues of Kāśyapa and Ānanda (Hamura City Designated Cultural Property).<ref name="hamura" />
== Access == - **By Train**: - 25-minute walk from {{ill|Fussa Station|ja|福生駅}} (JR Ome Line) or 12-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Hamura Station|ja|羽村駅}} - 8-minute taxi ride from Fussa Station - 25-minute walk or 8-minute taxi ride from {{ill|Higashi-Fussa Station|ja|東福生駅}} (JR Hachikō Line)
- **By Car**: - 25 minutes from Hachioji IC (Chūō Expressway) - 15 minutes from Hinode IC (Ken-Ō Expressway) - 20 minutes from Ōme IC (Ken-Ō Expressway)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite web|title=About Sōzen-ji|url=http://www.hamura-souzenji.com/about/|website=Sōzen-ji Official Website|accessdate=2021-03-02|ref={{sfnref|Official Site - About Sōzen-ji}}}} * {{cite book|title={{ill|New Edition Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}|volume=Vol. 108 Tama District No. 20|chapter=Kawasaki Village - Sōzen-ji, Yakushi Hall|url={{NDLJP|763992/52}}|publisher=Home Ministry Geography Bureau|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|New Edition Musashi Fudoki - Kawasaki Village}}}}

'''Sōzen-ji''' (宗禅寺) is a {{ill|Rinzai school|ja|臨済宗}} temple located in {{ill|Hamura, Tokyo|ja|羽村市}}, Tokyo. The temple’s mountain name is Iōzan (医王山).<ref name="overview"/> It is affiliated with {{ill|Kencho-ji|ja|建長寺}} in Kamakura as its head temple and {{ill|Kōtoku-ji (Akiruno)|ja|廣徳寺}} in Itsukaichi, Akiruno, as its branch temple.<ref name="official_site">Official website of Sōzen-ji.</ref>
Founded in 1615 (Genna 1),<ref name="overview"/> the temple welcomed the Zen master Gyokushū Genkyū from Kōtoku-ji as its founding abbot.<ref name="official_site"/> Originally located near the Tama River, it was relocated to its current site in 1695 due to flooding and the construction of the Tamagawa Aqueduct. The temple also serves as the 33rd site of the {{ill|Okutama Pilgrimage New Shikoku 88 Temples|ja|奥多摩霊場新四国八十八ヶ所霊場}}.

- **Yakushi Hall (Kawasaki Ippongi-dō)** Rebuilt during the Muromachi period, it is considered the oldest structure in Hamura City. - **Sansha-dō** (Hall of Three Shrines), **Jizō for Epidemic Dispersal**, **Kōshin Mound**, **Kyōzuka** (Sutra Mound), and **Bashō's Haiku Monument**.<ref name="a" />

Additional activities include Buddhist chanting, shakuhachi (bamboo flute), haiku gatherings, handmade udon workshops, newspaper salons, and a "Heart Counseling Room" offering tarot therapy.
